PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
Off Calendar Aug-12-2015 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Christina Yu-Ching Chen, for Petitioner Philip Chow
The Court requested information regarding the search for relatives and the capacity of the decedent at the time of the making of the will. Ms. Chen responded that since the last hearing she had engages a Shanghai search firm to research information regarding heirs in China. That company searched available records and placed notices in several newspapers, with no heir claimants coming forward or otherwise discovered. She stated that such research in China is difficult because there were no birth records maintained in China prior to 1949. She said that the decedent had left China for Hong Kong in 1946, and thereafter made a conscious effort to remain out of contact with her remaining family members in China. She said that the decedent over the last 25 years had always treated Philip Chow as her heir. When the Court replied that there was evidence to the contrary in the record, Ms. Chen stated that the decedent had been shrewd and used people when she wanted something by telling them she would leave them her house if they did what she wanted. She contended that the decedent's intention to keep up her relationship with Mr. Chow was demonstrated by the fact that after she sold one of her properties, she used the sales proceeds to buy two burial plots, one for herself and an adjoining one for Philip Chow. Regarding the decedent's testamentary capacity, Ms. Chen stated that while the decedent was already a conservatee at the time the will was executed, this did not necessarily mean that she lacked testamentary capacity. She argued that there was no showing of any lack of testamentary capacity until three months after the will was executed. She also noted that Mr. Chow was not present when the will was executed. The Court expressed concern regarding the short period between the execution of the will and the determination of lack of testamentary capacity, as failing mental health is usually gradual. Nevertheless, the Court was satisfied that a reasonable effort had been made to locate heirs, and no objections to the petition having been filed, the Court granted the Petition for Probate of Will and Letters testamentary (subject to review of the order). No bond was required. The Court set a Status Hearing re Closing of Estate for February 7, 2017, at 9:00 a.m., in Department 204.
Judge: Judge: Peter J. Busch. Clerk: Craig Blackstone/Ella Yip. Bailiff: Deputy Kabasares. (Not reported)

Off Calendar Jun-08-2015 Granted Per Written Order

PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
Off Calendar May-19-2015 Continued To Jul-14-15 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 204
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Christina Chen for and with (Petitioner) Phillip Chow.

The Court stated that this is a petition to appoint Mr. Chow as personal representative and to admit a will, that he is also a beneficiary, that the will was prepared while the decedent was under a conservatorship, that there is no evidence regarding testamentary capacity at the time, that there were concerns regarding the investigations during the conservatorship, elder abuse concerns regarding Mr. Chow's relationship with the conservatee, that there were earlier efforts by him to get her to sign over property, that there has been no notice to relatives and no indication as to what efforts have been made to track down siblings or descendants. Ms. Chen stated that P.C. 1870(c) reserves the right of the conservatee to make a will with no restrictions, that her intent to make Phillip an heir was well known, that Mr. Chow had assisted Ms. Cheung with her properties for 20 years, that APS came into the picture when Ms. Cheung had problems with her neighbors and that nothing has been out of the ordinary, just suspicious. The Court asked if Ms. Leung had been talked to in regards to filing for substituted judgment. Ms. Chen stated that the decedent repeatedly asked to make a will, that Ms. Leung and attorney Mr. Kato had no issues with this and that a petition for substituted judgment was ready to file with a declaration by Mr. Chow just before her death. Ms. Chen further stated that they have no names of heirs, that Ms. Cheung told her she was mistreated by her siblings and did not want anything to do with them, that they are in Shanghai, that Ms. Leung, APS and court investigator Melinda Gean have tried to find the names, that it appeared that Ms. Cheung did not want to remember their names and that Mr. Chow is all the family she had. The Court stated that it will continue the matter for Ms. Chen to solicit any additional information on evidence concerning testamentary capacity when Ms. Cheung was executing the will and information regarding the diligence of the search for heirs including who OmniTrace is and what they actually did. Ms. Chen stated that she may look for an heir finder in China to assist. The Petition for Probate of Will and for Letters Testamentary was continued to July 14, 2015 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204.

Judge: Peter J. Busch. Clerk: Leslie Gomez. Bailiff: Deputy Allen. (Not Reported)
